title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Where We Are




Ch. 4–8 — Data, splitting, recipes, workflows


Today (Ch. 9–10) — How do we know if any of it worked?


Ch. 11+ — Comparing and tuning models


Frame today as the hinge point of the course: everything before this was building a model; everything after this is improving a model. You can’t improve what you can’t measure.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Question Underneath Every Model




“Is this model actually good —


or did it just get lucky?”


This is the thesis of both chapters. Ch 9 gives us the ruler. Ch 10 makes sure we’re not measuring on a surface that’s tilted in our favor.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Two Chapters, One Throughline





Ch. 9
How do we measure it?
Metrics: RMSE, sensitivity, ROC/AUC — the instruments.



Ch. 10
How do we measure it honestly?
Resampling: cross-validation — measure the same thing many times.



You need a ruler before you can ask whether it’s calibrated.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Why Metrics, Not Vibes




A fitted model produces predictions. Predictions need a number that says how good they are.


That number depends on the business question — there is no universal “best” metric.


A churn model that misses real churners ≠ a churn model that over-flags loyal customers. Same accuracy. Very different cost.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Regression Metrics



reg_metrics <- metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)

reg_preds |> reg_metrics(truth = order_value_next, estimate = .pred)




metric
reads as
in dollars?




RMSE
typical error size
yes — penalizes big misses


R²
% variance explained
no — unitless, 0 to 1


MAE
typical error size
yes — robust to outliers





Emphasize: RMSE and MAE answer “how far off, on average” but RMSE punishes large misses harder because of the square. If your data has occasional huge orders, RMSE and MAE will tell different stories — that’s exercise 9.3.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Confusion Matrix



conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)






Actual: Yes


Actual: No


Pred: Yes


TP


FP


Pred: No


FN


TN




Every classification metric is built from these four cells.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Four Cells, Four Questions




Sensitivity — of true responders, how many did we catch?
Specificity — of true non-responders, how many did we correctly skip?
Precision — of who we targeted, how many actually responded?
Accuracy — overall, how often were we right? (careful — see next slide)

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Accuracy Trap




Direct-mail response rates: typically 3–8%.


A model that predicts “no” for every single prospect still scores 92–97% accuracy.


It is also completely useless for targeting.


Always pair accuracy with sensitivity / specificity.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
ROC Curve & AUC





roc_curve(test_preds, 
          truth = responded, 
          .pred_yes) |> 
  autoplot()
A single threshold (0.5) gives one sensitivity/specificity pair.
The ROC curve sweeps every threshold at once.



0.5coin flip


0.75quite good


1.0perfect

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Multiclass: Same Idea, More Classes




loyalty_tier: Bronze / Silver / Gold / Platinum


yardstick computes each metric per class (one-vs-rest), then averages.


Macro-average (default): every class counts equally — Platinum isn’t drowned out by Bronze, even though Bronze has far more customers.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Problem with One Measurement




Section 1 gave us a ROC AUC. One number, from one test set.


Would that number change if we’d split the data differently?


We don’t know — unless we measure it more than once.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Resubstitution: The Optimism Trap



What if we score the model on the data it was trained on?
train_preds <- augment(mail_fit, new_data = mail_train)
test_preds  <- augment(mail_fit, new_data = mail_test)



training (resubstitution)


test (held out)


inflated — model has memorized noise


honest — genuinely unseen data




The gap between these two is the bias resampling exists to detect and avoid.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
section: Why Not Just Reuse the Test Set?
text:
Why Not Just Reuse the Test Set?




The test set should be touched once — at the very end.


Use it repeatedly while comparing or tuning models, and you start fitting to it — same problem as resubstitution, one level up.


Resampling gets a trustworthy estimate using only training data.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
V-Fold Cross-Validation



mail_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, strata = responded)



Fold 1







Fold 2







Fold 3







⋮





Fold 10









Every row is held out exactly once. Train on 9 folds, score on the 10th, rotate.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Other Resampling Schemes






Method
Use it when…




V-fold CV
default choice, most marketing data


Repeated CV
small data, fold-to-fold noise is high


Bootstrap
need uncertainty estimates, very small data


Validation split
data is huge and CV is too slow


Rolling origin
time-ordered data — campaign cohorts by month

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Running It: fit_resamples()



cv_results <- fit_resamples(
  mail_wf,
  resamples = mail_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(cv_results)

Fits the same workflow 10 times — once per fold — and averages the results.

section: One Test Set vs. Ten Folds
text:
One Test Set vs. Ten Folds





Single test set


10-fold CV mean


one draw from a distribution


averages 10 draws


no sense of stability


comes with a standard error



The standard error tells you how much that estimate might wobble on a different split.

Parallel Processing — A Practical Note




Resampling fits the same workflow many times — an embarrassingly parallel task.


library(doParallel)
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(parallel::detectCores() - 1)
registerDoParallel(cl)


fit_resamples(), tune_grid(), and friends pick this up automatically once a backend is registered.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.1 Why Metrics, Not Just “It Fit”






Important



There is no single “best” metric. The right metric depends on the business question. For a churn model, missing a true churner (low sensitivity) might be far more costly than flagging a loyal customer by mistake (low specificity). Always pick metrics that match the marketing decision the model supports.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.2 Regression Metrics
We don’t have a regression model yet in this notebook, so we’ll fit one quickly — predicting order_value_next from a few predictors — purely to demonstrate the metrics.


Code
reg_spec <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm")

reg_rec <- recipe(
  order_value_next ~ income + freq_12mo + recency_days,
  data = mail_train
) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

reg_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(reg_rec) |> add_model(reg_spec)
reg_fit <- fit(reg_wf, data = mail_train)

reg_preds <- augment(reg_fit, new_data = mail_test)
reg_preds |> select(customer_id, order_value_next, .pred, .resid) |> head()



  





Code
reg_metrics <- metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)

reg_preds |>
  reg_metrics(truth = order_value_next, estimate = .pred)



  




4.2.1 RMSE (root mean squared error):
Average prediction error, in the original units (dollars here). Penalizes large errors more heavily because errors are squared before averaging.






NoteRMSE Formula



\[
RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
\]

Where

\(n\) is the number of observations,
\(y_i\) is the actual value, and
\(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value.
\((y_i - \hat{y}_i)\) = the residual (prediction error) for observation \(i\)

RMSE is in the same units as the outcome variable — dollars, in our case — making it directly interpretable.





4.2.2 R² (rsq):

Proportion of variance in the outcome explained by the model.
Ranges roughly 0–1; higher is better.
Useful for communicating “how much of next-order value our model explains” to stakeholders.







NoteR-Squared formula



\[
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}
\]
Where:

\(y_i\) = actual value for observation \(i\)
\(\hat{y}_i\) = predicted value for observation \(i\)
\(\bar{y}\) = mean of all actual values
\(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\) = total unexplained variation (residual sum of squares)
\(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2\) = total variation in the outcome (total sum of squares)





4.2.3 MAE (mean absolute error):

Average absolute error, less sensitive to outliers than RMSE.
If a few extreme spenders are skewing RMSE, compare it against MAE.







NoteMAE Formula



\[
MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i|
\]
Where:

\(n\) = number of observations
\(y_i\) = actual value for observation \(i\)
\(\hat{y}_i\) = predicted value for observation \(i\)
\(|y_i - \hat{y}_i|\) = absolute prediction error for observation \(i\)





Code
ggplot(reg_preds, aes(x = order_value_next, y = .pred)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#2E5266") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "tomato") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs. Actual Next-Order Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = perfect prediction",
    x = "Actual order value ($)",
    y = "Predicted order value ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.3 Binary Classification Metrics
Back to our responded (yes/no) model. The starting point for classification metrics is the confusion matrix: a 2×2 table of predicted vs. actual class.


Code
conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)


          Truth
Prediction yes  no
       yes   0   0
       no   31 569




Code
conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap")















NoteFrom Confusion Matrix to Metrics











Metric
Formula (informal)
Marketing question it answers




Sensitivity (recall)
TP / (TP + FN)
Of customers who would respond, how many did we catch?


Specificity
TN / (TN + FP)
Of customers who wouldn’t respond, how many did we correctly skip?


Precision (PPV)
TP / (TP + FP)
Of customers we targeted, how many actually responded?


Accuracy
(TP + TN) / Total
Overall, how often is the model right? (misleading with rare classes — see warning below)






4.3.1 Sensitivity (Recall)






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives — customers who responded and were correctly predicted to respond
\(FN\) = false negatives — customers who responded but were incorrectly predicted not to respond
\(TP + FN\) = total number of actual responders





4.3.2 Specificity






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
\]
Where:

\(TN\) = true negatives — customers who did not respond and were correctly predicted not to respond
\(FP\) = false positives — customers who did not respond but were incorrectly predicted to respond
\(TN + FP\) = total number of actual non-responders





4.3.3 Precision (PPV)






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives — correctly predicted responders
\(FP\) = false positives — non-responders incorrectly predicted as responders
\(TP + FP\) = total number of customers predicted to respond





4.3.4 Accuracy






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives
\(TN\) = true negatives
\(FP\) = false positives
\(FN\) = false negatives
\(TP + TN + FP + FN\) = total number of observations





4.3.5 F1 Score






NoteDetails



\[
F_1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Sensitivity}}
\]
Where:

\(\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}\)
\(\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\)
\(F_1\) is the harmonic mean of precision and sensitivity — useful when both matter equally and the classes are imbalanced





Code
class_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, f_meas)

test_preds |>
  class_metrics(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)



  









Warning



Accuracy is dangerous with imbalanced classes. Direct-mail response rates are often only 3–8%. A model that predicts “no” for everyone could still score 95%+ accuracy while being completely useless for targeting. Always check sensitivity/specificity alongside accuracy — this is exactly the kind of trap a stakeholder unfamiliar with the data could fall into.




4.3.6 ROC Curve and AUC
Sensitivity and specificity above were computed at a single classification threshold (0.5 by default). The ROC curve shows the sensitivity/specificity trade-off across every possible threshold, and AUC summarizes the whole curve in one number.


Code
roc_data <- test_preds |>
  roc_curve(truth = responded, .pred_yes)

auc_val <- test_preds |>
  roc_auc(truth = responded, .pred_yes) |>
  pull(.estimate)




Code
autoplot(roc_data) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.6,
    y = 0.2,
    label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc_val, 3)),
    size = 5,
    color = "#2E5266",
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curve — Direct-Mail Response Model",
    subtitle = "Closer to the top-left corner = better discrimination"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)















Tip



Rule of thumb. AUC = 0.5 means the model is no better than a coin flip. AUC = 1.0 is a perfect classifier (rare in practice). For direct-mail targeting, AUC > 0.65 is often usable; > 0.75 is quite good.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.4 Multiclass Classification Metrics
loyalty_tier (Bronze/Silver/Gold/Platinum) is a 4-level outcome. While we won’t build a full multiclass model here, it’s worth knowing how the binary metrics above generalize.


Code
# Demonstration only: predict loyalty_tier from age + income as a toy multiclass model
multi_rec <- recipe(loyalty_tier ~ age + income, data = mail_train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

multi_spec <- multinom_reg() |> set_engine("nnet")

multi_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(multi_rec) |> add_model(multi_spec)
multi_fit <- fit(multi_wf, data = mail_train)

multi_preds <- augment(multi_fit, new_data = mail_test)

multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(truth = loyalty_tier, estimate = .pred_class)



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  roc_auc(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    .pred_Bronze,
    .pred_Silver,
    .pred_Gold,
    .pred_Platinum
  ) |>
  pull(.estimate)


[1] 0.4817859








NoteHow Binary Metrics Generalize to Multiclass



With more than two classes, yardstick computes a metric per class (treating each as “this class” vs. “all others”), then averages across classes. The default is macro-averaging — each class counts equally, regardless of how many observations it has. This matters for loyalty_tier: Platinum customers are rare, but macro-averaging keeps them from being drowned out by the much larger Bronze group.



4.4.1 Estimator Argument (Optional)

Refer to Chapter 10



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro_weighted"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "micro"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  roc_auc(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    .pred_Bronze,
    .pred_Silver,
    .pred_Gold,
    .pred_Platinum,
    estimator = "macro_weighted"
  )

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
section: 5.2 Why Not Just Use the Test Set Repeatedly?
text:
5.2 Why Not Just Use the Test Set Repeatedly?
The test set should be touched once, at the very end, to report final performance. If we use it over and over while comparing models or tuning hyperparameters, we implicitly start fitting to it — defeating its purpose. Resampling lets us get reliable performance estimates using only the training data, keeping the test set untouched until final evaluation.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.3 Cross-Validation
The most common resampling scheme is v-fold (k-fold) cross-validation: split the training data into v roughly equal folds, train on v − 1 of them, evaluate on the held-out fold, and rotate until every fold has served as the validation set exactly once.


Code
set.seed(2024)
mail_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, strata = responded)
mail_folds



  





Code
# Look at one fold up close
mail_folds$splits[[1]]


<Analysis/Assess/Total>
<2160/240/2400>








NoteWhat vfold_cv() Just Built



mail_folds is a tibble of 10 rsplit objects. Each one knows which rows of mail_train are the analysis set (≈ 90%, used for fitting) and which are the assessment set (≈ 10%, used for scoring) for that fold. strata = responded keeps the response rate roughly constant across folds — same logic as stratifying the original train/test split in Week 2.



5.3.1 Visualizing the Fold Structure


Code
fold_viz <- map_dfr(seq_along(mail_folds$splits), function(i) {
  split <- mail_folds$splits[[i]]
  tibble(
    fold = i,
    row = 1:nrow(mail_train),
    role = if_else(
      row %in% split$in_id,
      "Analysis (train)",
      "Assessment (validate)"
    )
  )
})

ggplot(fold_viz, aes(x = row, y = factor(fold), fill = role)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Analysis (train)" = "#A9C0CC",
      "Assessment (validate)" = "#C0504D"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "10-Fold Cross-Validation Structure",
    subtitle = "Each row of the training data is held out exactly once",
    x = "Training set row index",
    y = "Fold",
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.4 Other Resampling Methods (Brief Tour)


Code
# Repeated CV: run 10-fold CV five times with different random splits
repeated_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, repeats = 5, strata = responded)
cat("Repeated CV total resamples:", nrow(repeated_folds), "\n")


Repeated CV total resamples: 50 


Code
# Bootstrap: sample WITH replacement, same size as training data
set.seed(2024)
boot_resamples <- bootstraps(mail_train, times = 25, strata = responded)
cat("Bootstrap resamples:", nrow(boot_resamples), "\n")


Bootstrap resamples: 25 


Code
# Validation set: a single held-out chunk carved from the training data
val_set <- validation_split(mail_train, prop = 0.80, strata = responded)
val_set



  









NoteWhich Resampling Method When?











Method
When to use it
Tradeoff




V-fold CV
Default choice for most marketing datasets
Good balance of bias and computation


Repeated CV
Smaller datasets where fold-to-fold variability is high
More stable estimates, v × repeats the compute


Bootstrap
Want a measure of estimate uncertainty, or very small data
Some observations never appear in assessment sets


Validation set
Very large data where CV is too slow
Only one estimate — less stable than CV


Rolling origin
Time-ordered data (e.g., monthly campaign cohorts)
Respects temporal order; no shuffling allowed

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.5 Estimating Performance with fit_resamples()
This is where it all comes together: fit the workflow on each fold’s analysis set, score it on the assessment set, and average the results.


Code
set.seed(2024)
cv_results <- fit_resamples(
  mail_wf,
  resamples = mail_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

cv_results



  





Code
collect_metrics(cv_results)



  









ImportantComparing the Single Test Set vs. Cross-Validated Estimate





Code
bind_rows(
  test_preds |>
    metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc)(
      truth = responded,
      estimate = .pred_class,
      .pred_yes
    ) |>
    mutate(source = "Single test set"),
  collect_metrics(cv_results) |>
    filter(.metric %in% c("accuracy", "roc_auc")) |>
    select(.metric, .estimate = mean) |>
    mutate(source = "10-fold CV mean")
) |>
  select(source, .metric, .estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = source, values_from = .estimate)



  



The single test-set number is one draw from a distribution of possible performance estimates. The cross-validated mean averages over 10 different draws, giving a more stable — and more honest — picture of how the model is likely to perform on new prospects. The standard error reported by collect_metrics() tells us how much that estimate might wobble.




Code
collect_metrics(cv_results, summarize = FALSE) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = id, y = .estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E5266", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(yintercept = mean(.estimate)),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "tomato"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC AUC by Fold",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = mean across all 10 folds",
    x = "Fold",
    y = "ROC AUC"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

5.6 Parallel Processing (Conceptual Note)


Code
# Resampling fits the same workflow many times — an embarrassingly parallel task.
# On a multi-core machine, register a parallel backend before fit_resamples():

# install.packages("doParallel")
library(doParallel)
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(parallel::detectCores() - 1)
registerDoParallel(cl)

cv_results <- fit_resamples(mail_wf, resamples = mail_folds)

stopCluster(cl)








Tip



We don’t run this chunk live in class (it depends on your machine’s core count), but know that fit_resamples(), tune_grid(), and friends all parallelize automatically once a backend is registered — useful once your resampling scheme + grid search gets large later in the course.

5.7 Saving Resampled Objects


Code
# Because we set save_pred = TRUE above, we can pull out every held-out prediction
cv_preds <- collect_predictions(cv_results)
cv_preds |> select(id, .row, responded, .pred_class, .pred_yes) |> head()



  





Code
cv_preds |>
  group_by(id) |>
  roc_curve(truth = responded, .pred_yes) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity, color = id)) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curves Across All 10 Folds",
    subtitle = "Spread between lines = fold-to-fold variability",
    color = "Fold"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")















Note



Why save predictions? With save_pred = TRUE, we get every out-of-fold prediction back, which lets us build diagnostic plots like the multi-fold ROC curve above, inspect specific misclassified prospects, or build custom metrics — all without ever touching the test set.

title: Module 3 - Exercise Answer Keys
section: 2.1 Exercise 9.1 — Confusion Matrix and Threshold Tradeoff
text:
2.1 Exercise 9.1 — Confusion Matrix and Threshold Tradeoff

2.1.1 Part A — Reading the Confusion Matrix


Code
cm <- conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)
cm


          Truth
Prediction yes  no
       yes   0   0
       no   31 569


Code
autoplot(cm, type = "heatmap")















NoteReading Each Cell



The confusion matrix is a 2×2 table of predicted class (rows) vs. actual class (columns). Here is how to read each cell in the context of a direct-mail campaign:








Cell
Name
What it means




Predicted “yes”, Actual “yes”
True Positive (TP)
We mailed them and they responded — a successful targeting decision


Predicted “yes”, Actual “no”
False Positive (FP)
We mailed them but they did not respond — wasted mailing cost


Predicted “no”, Actual “yes”
False Negative (FN)
We did not mail them but they would have responded — a missed opportunity


Predicted “no”, Actual “no”
True Negative (TN)
We correctly skipped them — money saved





Q4 — Which error costs more?
With a $5 mailing cost and $50 average order value:

A false positive costs $5 (we mailed someone who didn’t respond)
A false negative costs the lost profit margin on a $50 order — potentially $20–$40 depending on margins

In most direct-mail settings, false negatives are more costly — missing a genuine responder loses more revenue than the small cost of an unnecessary mailing. This is why marketers often lower the classification threshold below 0.5 to catch more true responders, accepting more false positives in exchange.


2.1.2 Part B — Threshold Tradeoff


Code
# At default threshold of 0.5
default_metrics <- test_preds |>
  metric_set(precision, sens)(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)

# At lower threshold of 0.3
preds_03 <- test_preds |>
  mutate(
    .pred_class_03 = factor(
      if_else(.pred_yes > 0.3, "yes", "no"),
      levels = c("yes", "no")
    )
  )

low_threshold_metrics <- preds_03 |>
  metric_set(precision, sens)(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class_03)

# Compare
bind_rows(
  default_metrics |> mutate(threshold = "0.5 (default)"),
  low_threshold_metrics |> mutate(threshold = "0.3 (lower)")
) |>
  select(threshold, .metric, .estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = threshold, values_from = .estimate)



  









NoteWhy Lowering the Threshold Increases Sensitivity



Lowering the threshold from 0.5 to 0.3 means we classify more customers as “yes” — including customers the model is only moderately confident about.

Sensitivity increases because we are now catching more of the true responders we previously missed (fewer false negatives)
Precision decreases because we are also adding more non-responders to the “yes” pile (more false positives)

This is the fundamental precision-sensitivity tradeoff: you can always catch more true responders by being less selective, but you pay for it with more wasted mailings. The ROC curve visualizes this tradeoff across every possible threshold simultaneously.

section: 2.2 Exercise 9.2 — ROC/AUC Interpretation and Custom metric_set()
text:
2.2 Exercise 9.2 — ROC/AUC Interpretation and Custom metric_set()

2.2.1 Part A — Interpreting AUC


Code
# Retrieve the AUC value
auc_val <- test_preds |>
  roc_auc(truth = responded, .pred_yes) |>
  pull(.estimate)

cat("AUC:", round(auc_val, 3), "\n")


AUC: 0.473 


Code
cat(
  "In plain English: the model ranks a true responder above a true",
  "non-responder",
  round(auc_val * 100, 1),
  "% of the time.\n"
)


In plain English: the model ranks a true responder above a true non-responder 47.3 % of the time.


Code
# Plot the ROC curve to answer Q3
roc_data <- test_preds |>
  roc_curve(truth = responded, .pred_yes)

autoplot(roc_data) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.6,
    y = 0.2,
    label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc_val, 3)),
    size = 5,
    color = "#2E5266",
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0.10,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "tomato",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.13,
    y = 0.05,
    label = "Top 10%",
    size = 3.5,
    color = "tomato"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curve — Direct-Mail Response Model",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line = operating at top 10% of prospects"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)















NoteReading the AUC



AUC = 0.473 means that if we randomly pick one customer who responded and one who did not, the model assigns a higher predicted probability to the responder about 47.3% of the time. This is well above the 0.65 threshold described as “usable for targeting” — a solid result for direct-mail.
Q3 — Operating at the top 10%: If your budget allows mailing only the top 10% of prospects (1 − specificity ≈ 0.10 on the x-axis), find that point on the ROC curve and read off the sensitivity — that tells you what fraction of all true responders you would reach with that budget.




2.2.2 Part B — Custom metric_set()


Code
custom_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec, f_meas)

test_preds |>
  custom_metrics(
    truth = responded,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    .pred_yes
  )



  









NoteWhich Two Metrics for a VP of Marketing?



The best two metrics to report to a non-technical stakeholder are:

roc_auc — it summarizes the model’s overall ability to rank responders above non-responders in a single number that doesn’t depend on any threshold choice. Easy to explain: “80% of the time the model correctly identifies who is more likely to respond.”
sens (sensitivity) — it directly answers the business question: “Of all the customers who would have responded, what fraction did we successfully target?” This maps directly to revenue recovered vs. revenue missed.

Why not accuracy? With a ~4.7% response rate, accuracy is inflated and misleading — a model that predicts “no” for everyone scores 95%+ accuracy while being useless. Always pair accuracy with sensitivity/specificity when classes are imbalanced.

title: Module 3 - Exercise Answer Keys
text:
2.4 Exercise 9.4 — Multiclass Averaging


Code
# Macro (default) — each class counts equally
macro_metrics <- multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro"
  )

# Macro-weighted — classes weighted by their frequency
macro_wt_metrics <- multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro_weighted"
  )

# Micro — treats all observations equally regardless of class
micro_metrics <- multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "micro"
  )

# Compare all three
bind_rows(
  macro_metrics |> mutate(estimator = "macro"),
  macro_wt_metrics |> mutate(estimator = "macro_weighted"),
  micro_metrics |> mutate(estimator = "micro")
) |>
  filter(.metric == "sens") |>
  select(estimator, .estimate) |>
  arrange(desc(.estimate))



  









NoteUnderstanding the Three Estimators



Q1 — Why micro looks more favorable: Micro-averaging treats every observation equally — since Bronze customers make up ~40% of the data and are easiest to predict (the largest group tends to dominate), micro-averaging is pulled upward by correct Bronze predictions, masking poor performance on rarer tiers.
Q2 — Protecting Platinum customers: Use macro-averaging — it gives Platinum customers the same weight as Bronze customers regardless of how few Platinum observations there are. This ensures the metric reflects model performance across all tiers equally, not just the most common ones.
Q3 — Most misleading for marketing: Micro-averaging is the most likely to give an overly optimistic picture. In a loyalty program, Platinum customers are typically the most valuable — if the model performs poorly on them, micro-averaging will hide that by averaging it away with many correct Bronze predictions.

title: Module 3 - Exercise Answer Keys
section: 3.1 Exercise 10.1 — Fold Count and Connecting Back to Part 1
text:
3.1 Exercise 10.1 — Fold Count and Connecting Back to Part 1

3.1.1 Part A — Change the Number of Folds


Code
# 5-fold CV
set.seed(2024)
folds_5 <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 5, strata = responded)

cv_5fold <- fit_resamples(
  mail_wf,
  resamples = folds_5,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

# Compare 5-fold vs 10-fold
bind_rows(
  collect_metrics(cv_results) |>
    filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
    select(.metric, mean, std_err) |>
    mutate(method = "10-fold CV"),
  collect_metrics(cv_5fold) |>
    filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
    select(.metric, mean, std_err) |>
    mutate(method = "5-fold CV")
) |>
  select(method, mean, std_err)



  









NoteWhat Happens to Standard Error as v Decreases?



With fewer folds, the standard error of the mean roc_auc typically increases. Here is why:

With 10 folds, we get 10 different performance estimates and average them — more estimates means a more stable average
With 5 folds, each assessment set is twice as large (≈ 480 rows instead of ≈ 240), which reduces within-fold variance, but we have fewer folds to average over, which increases across-fold variance

The net effect is usually a slightly less stable estimate with fewer folds. The trade-off: 5-fold CV is faster to compute (5 model fits instead of 10) but produces a noisier performance estimate.




3.1.2 Part B — Bridge to Part 1


Code
# Single test-set roc_auc from Part 1
single_test_auc <- test_preds |>
  roc_auc(truth = responded, .pred_yes)

# 5-fold CV mean roc_auc
cv5_auc <- collect_metrics(cv_5fold) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  select(mean, std_err)

# 10-fold CV mean roc_auc
cv10_auc <- collect_metrics(cv_results) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  select(mean, std_err)

tibble(
  source = c("Single test set", "5-fold CV", "10-fold CV"),
  roc_auc = c(single_test_auc$.estimate, cv5_auc$mean, cv10_auc$mean),
  std_err = c(NA, cv5_auc$std_err, cv10_auc$std_err)
)



  









NoteSingle Test Set vs. Cross-Validated Estimates



Q1 — Are they close? All three numbers should be in a similar range. Small differences are expected — the single test-set number came from one particular 80/20 split, while CV numbers average over multiple different splits of the training data.
Q2 — Which is more trustworthy? The cross-validated estimate is more trustworthy because:

It averages over multiple held-out sets rather than relying on one particular split that might be lucky or unlucky
It uses only the training data, keeping the test set completely untouched for a final honest evaluation

Q3 — Why does CV have a standard error but the single test set does not? The standard error measures how much the AUC varies across folds. With one test set, we have exactly one number — there is nothing to measure variance across. The standard error is what makes CV estimates more informative: it tells you not just what the performance is, but how stable that estimate is.

title: Module 3 - Exercise Answer Keys
Module 3 - Exercise Answer Keys
text:
3.3 Exercise 10.3 — Diagnose a High-Variance Fold


Code
# Identify the fold with the lowest AUC
fold_aucs <- collect_metrics(cv_results, summarize = FALSE) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  arrange(.estimate)

cat(
  "Fold with lowest AUC:",
  fold_aucs$id[1],
  "| AUC =",
  round(fold_aucs$.estimate[1], 3),
  "\n"
)


Fold with lowest AUC: Fold06 | AUC = 0.444 


Code
worst_fold <- fold_aucs$id[1]

# Pull that fold's predictions
worst_fold_preds <- cv_preds |>
  filter(id == worst_fold)

# Confusion matrix for the worst fold
conf_mat(worst_fold_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)


          Truth
Prediction yes  no
       yes   0   0
       no    9 231


Code
# Compare to overall confusion matrix
cat("\n--- Overall confusion matrix (full test set) ---\n")



--- Overall confusion matrix (full test set) ---


Code
conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)


          Truth
Prediction yes  no
       yes   0   0
       no   31 569


Code
# Compare sensitivity specifically
bind_rows(
  worst_fold_preds |>
    sens(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(source = paste("Worst fold:", worst_fold)),
  cv_preds |>
    sens(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(source = "All folds combined"),
  test_preds |>
    sens(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(source = "Single test set")
) |>
  select(source, .estimate)



  









NoteWhat to Look For in a High-Variance Fold



The fold with the lowest AUC often has one or more of these characteristics:

Fewer true responders in the assessment set — with only ~28 responders per fold on average (4.7% of 600 rows), random variation in who lands in a fold can meaningfully change metrics
Lower sensitivity — the model may have missed more responders in that fold than average
Similar specificity — the large non-responder group is stable across folds, so specificity tends to vary less

This is exactly why we use 10-fold CV rather than a single validation split: fold-to-fold variability reveals how sensitive our performance estimate is to which particular customers happen to be held out. A fold with unusually low AUC is a signal to investigate, not necessarily a reason to discard the model.

title: Machine Learning Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
This module covers the two essential questions every machine learning model must answer: how good is it? and how do we know that estimate is trustworthy?
Chapter 9 gives you the measurement instruments — regression metrics (RMSE, R², MAE) and classification metrics (confusion matrix, sensitivity, specificity, ROC/AUC) — using the yardstick package.
Chapter 10 explains why a single measurement isn’t enough, and introduces resampling (cross-validation, bootstrap) via the rsample package to get stable, honest performance estimates from the training data alone.
Work through the materials in the order listed below.

Where We Are

Ch. 11+ — Comparing and tuning models


Frame today as the hinge point of the course: everything before this was building a model; everything after this is improving a model. You can’t improve what you can’t measure.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Question Underneath Every Model




“Is this model actually good —


or did it just get lucky?”


This is the thesis of both chapters. Ch 9 gives us the ruler. Ch 10 makes sure we’re not measuring on a surface that’s tilted in our favor.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Two Chapters, One Throughline





Ch. 9
How do we measure it?
Metrics: RMSE, sensitivity, ROC/AUC — the instruments.



Ch. 10
How do we measure it honestly?
Resampling: cross-validation — measure the same thing many times.



You need a ruler before you can ask whether it’s calibrated.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Why Metrics, Not Vibes




A fitted model produces predictions. Predictions need a number that says how good they are.


That number depends on the business question — there is no universal “best” metric.


A churn model that misses real churners ≠ a churn model that over-flags loyal customers. Same accuracy. Very different cost.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Regression Metrics



reg_metrics <- metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)

reg_preds |> reg_metrics(truth = order_value_next, estimate = .pred)




metric
reads as
in dollars?




RMSE
typical error size
yes — penalizes big misses


R²
% variance explained
no — unitless, 0 to 1


MAE
typical error size
yes — robust to outliers





Emphasize: RMSE and MAE answer “how far off, on average” but RMSE punishes large misses harder because of the square. If your data has occasional huge orders, RMSE and MAE will tell different stories — that’s exercise 9.3.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Confusion Matrix



conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)






Actual: Yes


Actual: No


Pred: Yes


TP


FP


Pred: No


FN


TN




Every classification metric is built from these four cells.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Four Cells, Four Questions




Sensitivity — of true responders, how many did we catch?
Specificity — of true non-responders, how many did we correctly skip?
Precision — of who we targeted, how many actually responded?
Accuracy — overall, how often were we right? (careful — see next slide)

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Accuracy Trap




Direct-mail response rates: typically 3–8%.


A model that predicts “no” for every single prospect still scores 92–97% accuracy.


It is also completely useless for targeting.


Always pair accuracy with sensitivity / specificity.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
ROC Curve & AUC





roc_curve(test_preds, 
          truth = responded, 
          .pred_yes) |> 
  autoplot()
A single threshold (0.5) gives one sensitivity/specificity pair.
The ROC curve sweeps every threshold at once.



0.5coin flip


0.75quite good


1.0perfect

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Multiclass: Same Idea, More Classes




loyalty_tier: Bronze / Silver / Gold / Platinum


yardstick computes each metric per class (one-vs-rest), then averages.


Macro-average (default): every class counts equally — Platinum isn’t drowned out by Bronze, even though Bronze has far more customers.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Problem with One Measurement




Section 1 gave us a ROC AUC. One number, from one test set.


Would that number change if we’d split the data differently?


We don’t know — unless we measure it more than once.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Resubstitution: The Optimism Trap



What if we score the model on the data it was trained on?
train_preds <- augment(mail_fit, new_data = mail_train)
test_preds  <- augment(mail_fit, new_data = mail_test)



training (resubstitution)


test (held out)


inflated — model has memorized noise


honest — genuinely unseen data




The gap between these two is the bias resampling exists to detect and avoid.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
section: Why Not Just Reuse the Test Set?
text:
Why Not Just Reuse the Test Set?




The test set should be touched once — at the very end.


Use it repeatedly while comparing or tuning models, and you start fitting to it — same problem as resubstitution, one level up.


Resampling gets a trustworthy estimate using only training data.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
V-Fold Cross-Validation



mail_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, strata = responded)



Fold 1







Fold 2







Fold 3







⋮





Fold 10









Every row is held out exactly once. Train on 9 folds, score on the 10th, rotate.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
The Other Resampling Schemes






Method
Use it when…




V-fold CV
default choice, most marketing data


Repeated CV
small data, fold-to-fold noise is high


Bootstrap
need uncertainty estimates, very small data


Validation split
data is huge and CV is too slow


Rolling origin
time-ordered data — campaign cohorts by month

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Running It: fit_resamples()



cv_results <- fit_resamples(
  mail_wf,
  resamples = mail_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(cv_results)

Fits the same workflow 10 times — once per fold — and averages the results.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
section: One Test Set vs. Ten Folds
text:
One Test Set vs. Ten Folds





Single test set


10-fold CV mean


one draw from a distribution


averages 10 draws


no sense of stability


comes with a standard error



The standard error tells you how much that estimate might wobble on a different split.

text:
Parallel Processing — A Practical Note




Resampling fits the same workflow many times — an embarrassingly parallel task.


library(doParallel)
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(parallel::detectCores() - 1)
registerDoParallel(cl)


fit_resamples(), tune_grid(), and friends pick this up automatically once a backend is registered.

text:
Today, in One Line




Pick the right ruler.


Then measure with it more than once.

title: Module 3 Slides — Model Evaluation & Resampling
text:
Next Week




Ch. 11 — Comparing Models with Resampling


Now that we can measure one model honestly — how do we compare several models on equal footing?


Bring a model you’ve been curious about. We’ll resample it.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.1 Why Metrics, Not Just “It Fit”






Important



There is no single “best” metric. The right metric depends on the business question. For a churn model, missing a true churner (low sensitivity) might be far more costly than flagging a loyal customer by mistake (low specificity). Always pick metrics that match the marketing decision the model supports.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.2 Regression Metrics
We don’t have a regression model yet in this notebook, so we’ll fit one quickly — predicting order_value_next from a few predictors — purely to demonstrate the metrics.


Code
reg_spec <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm")

reg_rec <- recipe(
  order_value_next ~ income + freq_12mo + recency_days,
  data = mail_train
) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

reg_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(reg_rec) |> add_model(reg_spec)
reg_fit <- fit(reg_wf, data = mail_train)

reg_preds <- augment(reg_fit, new_data = mail_test)
reg_preds |> select(customer_id, order_value_next, .pred, .resid) |> head()



  





Code
reg_metrics <- metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)

reg_preds |>
  reg_metrics(truth = order_value_next, estimate = .pred)



  




4.2.1 RMSE (root mean squared error):
Average prediction error, in the original units (dollars here). Penalizes large errors more heavily because errors are squared before averaging.






NoteRMSE Formula



\[
RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
\]

Where

\(n\) is the number of observations,
\(y_i\) is the actual value, and
\(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value.
\((y_i - \hat{y}_i)\) = the residual (prediction error) for observation \(i\)

RMSE is in the same units as the outcome variable — dollars, in our case — making it directly interpretable.





4.2.2 R² (rsq):

Proportion of variance in the outcome explained by the model.
Ranges roughly 0–1; higher is better.
Useful for communicating “how much of next-order value our model explains” to stakeholders.







NoteR-Squared formula



\[
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}
\]
Where:

\(y_i\) = actual value for observation \(i\)
\(\hat{y}_i\) = predicted value for observation \(i\)
\(\bar{y}\) = mean of all actual values
\(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\) = total unexplained variation (residual sum of squares)
\(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2\) = total variation in the outcome (total sum of squares)





4.2.3 MAE (mean absolute error):

Average absolute error, less sensitive to outliers than RMSE.
If a few extreme spenders are skewing RMSE, compare it against MAE.







NoteMAE Formula



\[
MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|y_i - \hat{y}_i|
\]
Where:

\(n\) = number of observations
\(y_i\) = actual value for observation \(i\)
\(\hat{y}_i\) = predicted value for observation \(i\)
\(|y_i - \hat{y}_i|\) = absolute prediction error for observation \(i\)





Code
ggplot(reg_preds, aes(x = order_value_next, y = .pred)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#2E5266") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "tomato") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs. Actual Next-Order Value",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = perfect prediction",
    x = "Actual order value ($)",
    y = "Predicted order value ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.3 Binary Classification Metrics
Back to our responded (yes/no) model. The starting point for classification metrics is the confusion matrix: a 2×2 table of predicted vs. actual class.


Code
conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)


          Truth
Prediction yes  no
       yes   0   0
       no   31 569




Code
conf_mat(test_preds, truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
  autoplot(type = "heatmap")















NoteFrom Confusion Matrix to Metrics











Metric
Formula (informal)
Marketing question it answers




Sensitivity (recall)
TP / (TP + FN)
Of customers who would respond, how many did we catch?


Specificity
TN / (TN + FP)
Of customers who wouldn’t respond, how many did we correctly skip?


Precision (PPV)
TP / (TP + FP)
Of customers we targeted, how many actually responded?


Accuracy
(TP + TN) / Total
Overall, how often is the model right? (misleading with rare classes — see warning below)






4.3.1 Sensitivity (Recall)






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives — customers who responded and were correctly predicted to respond
\(FN\) = false negatives — customers who responded but were incorrectly predicted not to respond
\(TP + FN\) = total number of actual responders





4.3.2 Specificity






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
\]
Where:

\(TN\) = true negatives — customers who did not respond and were correctly predicted not to respond
\(FP\) = false positives — customers who did not respond but were incorrectly predicted to respond
\(TN + FP\) = total number of actual non-responders





4.3.3 Precision (PPV)






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives — correctly predicted responders
\(FP\) = false positives — non-responders incorrectly predicted as responders
\(TP + FP\) = total number of customers predicted to respond





4.3.4 Accuracy






NoteDetails



\[
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
\]
Where:

\(TP\) = true positives
\(TN\) = true negatives
\(FP\) = false positives
\(FN\) = false negatives
\(TP + TN + FP + FN\) = total number of observations





4.3.5 F1 Score






NoteDetails



\[
F_1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Sensitivity}}
\]
Where:

\(\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}\)
\(\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}\)
\(F_1\) is the harmonic mean of precision and sensitivity — useful when both matter equally and the classes are imbalanced





Code
class_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, f_meas)

test_preds |>
  class_metrics(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class)



  









Warning



Accuracy is dangerous with imbalanced classes. Direct-mail response rates are often only 3–8%. A model that predicts “no” for everyone could still score 95%+ accuracy while being completely useless for targeting. Always check sensitivity/specificity alongside accuracy — this is exactly the kind of trap a stakeholder unfamiliar with the data could fall into.




4.3.6 ROC Curve and AUC
Sensitivity and specificity above were computed at a single classification threshold (0.5 by default). The ROC curve shows the sensitivity/specificity trade-off across every possible threshold, and AUC summarizes the whole curve in one number.


Code
roc_data <- test_preds |>
  roc_curve(truth = responded, .pred_yes)

auc_val <- test_preds |>
  roc_auc(truth = responded, .pred_yes) |>
  pull(.estimate)




Code
autoplot(roc_data) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.6,
    y = 0.2,
    label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc_val, 3)),
    size = 5,
    color = "#2E5266",
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curve — Direct-Mail Response Model",
    subtitle = "Closer to the top-left corner = better discrimination"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)















Tip



Rule of thumb. AUC = 0.5 means the model is no better than a coin flip. AUC = 1.0 is a perfect classifier (rare in practice). For direct-mail targeting, AUC > 0.65 is often usable; > 0.75 is quite good.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
4.4 Multiclass Classification Metrics
loyalty_tier (Bronze/Silver/Gold/Platinum) is a 4-level outcome. While we won’t build a full multiclass model here, it’s worth knowing how the binary metrics above generalize.


Code
# Demonstration only: predict loyalty_tier from age + income as a toy multiclass model
multi_rec <- recipe(loyalty_tier ~ age + income, data = mail_train) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

multi_spec <- multinom_reg() |> set_engine("nnet")

multi_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(multi_rec) |> add_model(multi_spec)
multi_fit <- fit(multi_wf, data = mail_train)

multi_preds <- augment(multi_fit, new_data = mail_test)

multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(truth = loyalty_tier, estimate = .pred_class)



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  roc_auc(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    .pred_Bronze,
    .pred_Silver,
    .pred_Gold,
    .pred_Platinum
  ) |>
  pull(.estimate)


[1] 0.4817859








NoteHow Binary Metrics Generalize to Multiclass



With more than two classes, yardstick computes a metric per class (treating each as “this class” vs. “all others”), then averages across classes. The default is macro-averaging — each class counts equally, regardless of how many observations it has. This matters for loyalty_tier: Platinum customers are rare, but macro-averaging keeps them from being drowned out by the much larger Bronze group.



4.4.1 Estimator Argument (Optional)

Refer to Chapter 10



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "macro_weighted"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  metric_set(accuracy, sens)(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    estimate = .pred_class,
    estimator = "micro"
  )



  



Code
multi_preds |>
  roc_auc(
    truth = loyalty_tier,
    .pred_Bronze,
    .pred_Silver,
    .pred_Gold,
    .pred_Platinum,
    estimator = "macro_weighted"
  )

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
section: 5.2 Why Not Just Use the Test Set Repeatedly?
text:
5.2 Why Not Just Use the Test Set Repeatedly?
The test set should be touched once, at the very end, to report final performance. If we use it over and over while comparing models or tuning hyperparameters, we implicitly start fitting to it — defeating its purpose. Resampling lets us get reliable performance estimates using only the training data, keeping the test set untouched until final evaluation.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.3 Cross-Validation
The most common resampling scheme is v-fold (k-fold) cross-validation: split the training data into v roughly equal folds, train on v − 1 of them, evaluate on the held-out fold, and rotate until every fold has served as the validation set exactly once.


Code
set.seed(2024)
mail_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, strata = responded)
mail_folds



  





Code
# Look at one fold up close
mail_folds$splits[[1]]


<Analysis/Assess/Total>
<2160/240/2400>








NoteWhat vfold_cv() Just Built



mail_folds is a tibble of 10 rsplit objects. Each one knows which rows of mail_train are the analysis set (≈ 90%, used for fitting) and which are the assessment set (≈ 10%, used for scoring) for that fold. strata = responded keeps the response rate roughly constant across folds — same logic as stratifying the original train/test split in Week 2.



5.3.1 Visualizing the Fold Structure


Code
fold_viz <- map_dfr(seq_along(mail_folds$splits), function(i) {
  split <- mail_folds$splits[[i]]
  tibble(
    fold = i,
    row = 1:nrow(mail_train),
    role = if_else(
      row %in% split$in_id,
      "Analysis (train)",
      "Assessment (validate)"
    )
  )
})

ggplot(fold_viz, aes(x = row, y = factor(fold), fill = role)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Analysis (train)" = "#A9C0CC",
      "Assessment (validate)" = "#C0504D"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "10-Fold Cross-Validation Structure",
    subtitle = "Each row of the training data is held out exactly once",
    x = "Training set row index",
    y = "Fold",
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.4 Other Resampling Methods (Brief Tour)


Code
# Repeated CV: run 10-fold CV five times with different random splits
repeated_folds <- vfold_cv(mail_train, v = 10, repeats = 5, strata = responded)
cat("Repeated CV total resamples:", nrow(repeated_folds), "\n")


Repeated CV total resamples: 50 


Code
# Bootstrap: sample WITH replacement, same size as training data
set.seed(2024)
boot_resamples <- bootstraps(mail_train, times = 25, strata = responded)
cat("Bootstrap resamples:", nrow(boot_resamples), "\n")


Bootstrap resamples: 25 


Code
# Validation set: a single held-out chunk carved from the training data
val_set <- validation_split(mail_train, prop = 0.80, strata = responded)
val_set



  









NoteWhich Resampling Method When?











Method
When to use it
Tradeoff




V-fold CV
Default choice for most marketing datasets
Good balance of bias and computation


Repeated CV
Smaller datasets where fold-to-fold variability is high
More stable estimates, v × repeats the compute


Bootstrap
Want a measure of estimate uncertainty, or very small data
Some observations never appear in assessment sets


Validation set
Very large data where CV is too slow
Only one estimate — less stable than CV


Rolling origin
Time-ordered data (e.g., monthly campaign cohorts)
Respects temporal order; no shuffling allowed

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.5 Estimating Performance with fit_resamples()
This is where it all comes together: fit the workflow on each fold’s analysis set, score it on the assessment set, and average the results.


Code
set.seed(2024)
cv_results <- fit_resamples(
  mail_wf,
  resamples = mail_folds,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

cv_results



  





Code
collect_metrics(cv_results)



  









ImportantComparing the Single Test Set vs. Cross-Validated Estimate





Code
bind_rows(
  test_preds |>
    metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc)(
      truth = responded,
      estimate = .pred_class,
      .pred_yes
    ) |>
    mutate(source = "Single test set"),
  collect_metrics(cv_results) |>
    filter(.metric %in% c("accuracy", "roc_auc")) |>
    select(.metric, .estimate = mean) |>
    mutate(source = "10-fold CV mean")
) |>
  select(source, .metric, .estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = source, values_from = .estimate)



  



The single test-set number is one draw from a distribution of possible performance estimates. The cross-validated mean averages over 10 different draws, giving a more stable — and more honest — picture of how the model is likely to perform on new prospects. The standard error reported by collect_metrics() tells us how much that estimate might wobble.




Code
collect_metrics(cv_results, summarize = FALSE) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = id, y = .estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E5266", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(yintercept = mean(.estimate)),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "tomato"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "ROC AUC by Fold",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = mean across all 10 folds",
    x = "Fold",
    y = "ROC AUC"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.6 Parallel Processing (Conceptual Note)


Code
# Resampling fits the same workflow many times — an embarrassingly parallel task.
# On a multi-core machine, register a parallel backend before fit_resamples():

# install.packages("doParallel")
library(doParallel)
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(parallel::detectCores() - 1)
registerDoParallel(cl)

cv_results <- fit_resamples(mail_wf, resamples = mail_folds)

stopCluster(cl)








Tip



We don’t run this chunk live in class (it depends on your machine’s core count), but know that fit_resamples(), tune_grid(), and friends all parallelize automatically once a backend is registered — useful once your resampling scheme + grid search gets large later in the course.

title: Module 3 Codebook — Model Evaluation and Resampling
text:
5.7 Saving Resampled Objects


Code
# Because we set save_pred = TRUE above, we can pull out every held-out prediction
cv_preds <- collect_predictions(cv_results)
cv_preds |> select(id, .row, responded, .pred_class, .pred_yes) |> head()



  





Code
cv_preds |>
  group_by(id) |>
  roc_curve(truth = responded, .pred_yes) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity, color = id)) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60") +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curves Across All 10 Folds",
    subtitle = "Spread between lines = fold-to-fold variability",
    color = "Fold"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")















Note



Why save predictions? With save_pred = TRUE, we get every out-of-fold prediction back, which lets us build diagnostic plots like the multi-fold ROC curve above, inspect specific misclassified prospects, or build custom metrics — all without ever touching the test set.

section: 6.1 ✏️ Exercise 9.1 — Confusion Matrix and Threshold Tradeoff
text:

text:
6.8 ✏️ Exercise 10.4 — Resubstitution Gap and Model Flexibility
Part A — Observe the Gap
Refit mail_wf after changing lr_spec to a random forest:


Code
rf_spec <- rand_forest() |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_mode("classification")

rf_wf <- mail_wf |> update_model(rf_spec)

rf_fit <- fit(rf_wf, data = mail_train)


Compare resubstitution accuracy (on mail_train) to held-out accuracy (on mail_test) for the random forest. Then do the same for the original Lasso logistic regression from the notebook.


Code
# Random forest: resubstitution vs. held-out
rf_train_preds <- augment(rf_fit, new_data = mail_train)
rf_test_preds  <- augment(rf_fit, new_data = mail_test)

bind_rows(
  rf_train_preds |> accuracy(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(model = "Random Forest", data = "train (resubstitution)"),
  rf_test_preds  |> accuracy(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(model = "Random Forest", data = "test (held out)"),
  train_preds    |> accuracy(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(model = "Lasso LR",      data = "train (resubstitution)"),
  test_preds     |> accuracy(truth = responded, estimate = .pred_class) |>
    mutate(model = "Lasso LR",      data = "test (held out)")
) |>
  select(model, data, .estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = data, values_from = .estimate) |>
  mutate(gap = `train (resubstitution)` - `test (held out)`)
